intent: INVERSE of scenario 9 (catalog #33, KEY ORDERING TRAP). A never rule placed AFTER an always rule on the same syscall. First-match-wins (f3 section 2.3) means the always rule records uid=0 execve before the never is consulted, so the never is INERT and does NOT suppress. Catches a model that treats any never as suppressive regardless of position.

rules.note: FIRST in load order; records ALL execve including uid=0. Full HIGH volume stands; the later never rule does NOT reduce it.

INERT: placed AFTER the always rule, so first-match-wins means the always already recorded uid=0 execve before this never is consulted. It SUPPRESSES NOTHING here (it would only matter for syscalls the always rule does not catch first). direction=inert (NOT suppressive; the never is dead for this traffic). Contrast scenario 9 (never ABOVE always => suppressive).

cost_band.note: FULL always band; the trailing never is INERT (suppresses nothing). NO reduction, unlike scenario 9.

suspected_trap: KEY CORRECTNESS TRAP (inverse of scenario 9). The never rule is placed AFTER the always rule on the same syscall (execve). Because rules evaluate in load order with FIRST-match-wins (f3 section 2.3), the always rule (line 1) records uid=0 execve BEFORE the never rule (line 2) is ever consulted, so the never rule is INERT -- it SUPPRESSES NOTHING. The correct net cost is the FULL always band ($9.12/mo typical), NO reduction. A naive impl that treats ANY never/exclude rule as suppressive regardless of POSITION wrongly subtracts the uid=0 slice and under-states cost. Direction here is INERT (not suppressive), the exact opposite of scenario 9 where the same two rules in the opposite order DO suppress. Position is load-bearing: never-AFTER-always does NOT suppress.
